data.title: Alamos Gold Wants To Satisfy The Market With More Value
data.description:
Alamos Gold Inc.: The Opportunity in the Gold Stock Market

In the current upward trend of the gold price, an investment in the NYSE-listed shares of Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI), a Canadian gold producer with operations in North America and Mexico, appears to be a sensible move. In addition to the record price of gold, a combination of other factors is likely to drive valuations even higher, thereby reinforcing the strong positive correlation with rising gold prices.

The Favourable Context of the Bullish Gold Price

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) has risen by 72% in the last twelve months and by 8% since the beginning of 2026, currently standing at $4,668.387 per troy ounce. Macroeconomic factors such as renewed geopolitical tensions—particularly the conflict between the US and the EU elite over Greenland's mineral resources, fueled by Macron's proposal for a massive trade offensive in response to Trump's threat of a 10 percent tariff on EU products—are driving demand for gold as a safe haven. This demand is now further amplified by perceived financial risks in the US banking system.

As Yahoo Finance reported last Wednesday (source already linked just above), Ulrike Hoffmann-Burchardi, CIO Americas and Global Head of Equities at UBS Global Wealth Management, issued a remarkable gold price forecast of $5,000 per ounce for the coming months, or even $5,400 per ounce.

A Solid Positive Correlation Between Alamos and Gold

The positive correlation between the NYSE shares of Alamos Gold Inc. (symbol: AGI) and changes in the gold price is illustrated by the dark yellow curve in the second box below the main chart. This curve shows AGI's return relative to the gold price over the past year. The dark yellow area in this box has generally been positive.

This suggests that AGI shares, on average, tend to be bullish when the price of gold rises and bearish when the price of gold falls, regardless of the returns on AGI shares and the price of gold over the past year (as these returns may well vary widely despite the positive correlation between the two securities). This pattern arises because fluctuations in the gold price influence market expectations about AGI's future profitability, which, in turn, plays a key role in shaping market sentiment on North American stock exchanges.

Alamos Gold Inc.: Canadian Gold Producer in America

In its production report for the fourth quarter and full year 2025 dated January 14, 2026 (hereinafter referred to as the “AGI Production Report”), Alamos Gold Inc. describes itself as follows:

Alamos is a Canadian-based intermediate gold producer with diversified production from three operations in North America. This includes the Young-Davidson mine and Island Gold District in northern Ontario, Canada, and the Mulatos District in Sonora State, Mexico. Additionally, the Company has a strong portfolio of growth projects, including the Phase 3+ Expansion at Island Gold, and the Lynn Lake project in Manitoba, Canada. […].

Alamos Gold Inc. is the sole owner of the following gold mines:

  • Young-Davidson Mine, an underground gold mine, 75 km southeast of Timmins, Ontario, Canada.
  • Island Gold Mine, an underground gold mine, 10 km southeast of Dubreuilville, Ontario, Canada.
  • Mulatos Mine, an open-pit leaching gold mine, 172 km northeast of Ciudad Obregón, Sonora, Mexico.
  • Lynn Lake Project, open-pit feasibility study, proposed gold and silver mine, 8 km northeast of Lynn Lake, Manitoba, Canada.

Key findings of the feasibility study—2023 forecast (according to the Alamos Gold website): The Lynn Lake project is projected to achieve an average annual production of 176,000 ounces of gold over the first 10 years. Based on our experience with other gold mining projects, Lynn Lake will enable low-cost gold production with all-in sustaining costs (AISC) of US$699 per ounce. Mineral resource information is also available on the website. The forecast is based on proven and probable mineral reserves of 3,322,000 ounces and measured and indicated mineral resources of 587,000 ounces. Lynn Lake could sell its first gold in early 2029. A higher initial capital cost (as seen in the earnings call transcript: Alamos Gold Q3 2025 from Investing.com), primarily due to inflation in recent years, does not seem to deter the company, which, on the contrary, has not reduced shareholder value programs since October 2025 and continues to have a strong focus on returning to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. Existing liquidity is ample, and multi-year cash flows - assuming the gold price remains at its current peak - will be robust, and these two things lead us to believe that banks will be willing to extend existing credit lines or provide new loans if necessary.

Alamos Gold Q4 & 2025 Production: Trends & Drivers

Gold production did not increase from the previous quarter but remained at the high level of Q3 2025. This interruption of growth disappointed the market, but we see no reason to overly penalize AGI because of this.

Although the company finished 2025 below its forecasts, this is a minor, temporary deviation attributable to uncontrollable external factors, particularly the extreme weather conditions in December. Despite these disruptions, the company's business remains unaffected, and it is well-positioned to benefit from the ongoing, record-breaking gold price rally.

John A. McCluskey, President and CEO of Alamos Gold Inc., commented on the production results for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025 as follows:

Our fourth quarter production totaled 141,500 ounces, consistent with the third quarter but below guidance due to lower than planned production from our Canadian operations. Mining and processing rates were lower than expected due to severe winter weather late in December which impacted access to the sites, as well as other operational challenges. Despite these challenges, we delivered a record quarter and year financially and made significant progress on our growth initiatives,

The company's production report indicates that while there are temporary difficulties, these will turn into upward potential once they are resolved.

From Alamos Gold's production report, we understand that:

Young-Davidson is on track to meet its gold production forecasts again as issues related to winter storms have been resolved, the damaged underground paste plug facility has been repaired, and capacity expansion through a new ore shaft will be completed in early 2026.

Island Gold sees operational benefits on the horizon, as the effects of the October 2025 earthquake have been overcome and the processing plant capacity is increased to 20,000 tonnes per day, leading to significant savings in operating costs, particularly in energy consumption.

Despite increased targets, production in the Mulatos district met the annual forecasts for 2025, underlining Mulatos' strong performance, with La Yaqui Grande exceeding planned results.

Young-Davidson, Island Gold, and Mulatos' “La Yaqui Grande” inspire hope for optimism regarding future productions.

Production Outlook

In his comments on the 2025 production results, John A. McCluskey explained his vision for the future growth of gold production:

From an operational perspective, this past year was not reflective of our long-term track record, nor our strong outlook. We expect a substantial improvement from our operations in 2026 and low-cost growth from the Island Gold District to drive production significantly higher over the next several years. As we will be outlining in the Island Gold District Expansion Study next month, we expect this growth to continue with a clear path to one million ounces of annual production by the end of the decade,

Financial Figures as of the End of 2025

Despite the aforementioned operational setbacks, Alamos Gold achieved record sales of $568 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 and record annual sales of $1.8 billion. These figures would be even more impressive if the realized gold price were not capped, as the company states, by the following:

the gold prepayment facility executed in July 2024 based on the prepaid price of $2,524 per ounce.

Margin improvement and cash flow were robust, allowing Alamos to achieve the major improvements that excite the market because they are synonymous with growth: the company ended the year with $623 million in cash and cash equivalents (up 35% sequentially and 90% year-over-year) and reduced its outstanding debt to $200 million. Alamos had sufficient free cash flow to fund its shareholder value enhancement program and market returns. For the full year, the company paid approximately $42.2 million in quarterly dividends, or $0.025 per share (the most recent payment was made on December 18, 2025). Also, 1,326,929 shares were repurchased for $38.8 million, resulting in a total payout to shareholders of $81 million for 2025, as we can see from the company's report.

For Investors: What to Expect from Alamos Gold

Resolving the operational issues will pave the way for higher production than projected for 2025, while costs will decrease by spreading fixed costs over a larger base and achieving economies of scale. Analysts also predict that the price of gold could rise to $5,400 per ounce in the coming months. These factors, together with the gradual phasing-out of half of Argonaut's legacy gold hedging transactions in 2026, increase the balance sheet's potential to benefit from a record rise in the gold price. Strictly speaking, the balance sheet and shareholder value must not be in worse shape at the end of 2026 than they were in 2025.

This exposure will be strengthened as the company continues to seek to acquire the remaining Argonaut legacy hedging positions, totaling 100,000 ounces for the period from late 2026 to early 2027, out of a total of 330,000 ounces, as 230,000 ounces have already been eliminated.

Alamos Gold Inc. on the NYSE

Alamos Gold Inc.’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “AGI” were trading at $38.90/share, giving it a market capitalization of $16.33 billion, as of this writing.

The stock offers a Div Rate (FWD) of $0.10, leading to a Yield (FWD) of 0.26%, according to Seeking Alpha.

Technical Analysis

The current stock price is 18.3% above its 52-week moving average of $31.775 (range: $19.68 to $43.87). The following structure—the 20-day moving average above the 50-day moving average, which in turn is above the 200-day moving average—suggests that AGI stock is in a sustained uptrend in both the short and long term.

Valuation

The company states:

Increased shareholder returns: repurchased 928,729 shares in November at a cost of $28.8 million, or $30.96 per share. For the full year, 1,326,929 shares were repurchased for $38.8 million. Including dividends and share buybacks, a record $81 million was returned to shareholders in 2025

The average repurchase price was $30.96 per share in November 2025 and $29.24 per share for the full year 2025. From December 31, 2024, to December 31, 2025, AGI's average share price on the NYSE was approximately $28.24. The increase in market capitalization was strong, driven by the record increase in the price of gold. The company readily bought back its own shares, resulting in higher expenses and a higher average cash payout per share. Alamos Gold Inc. will have more funds available for share repurchases and maintaining dividend payments going forward as the gold business is on a solid footing, thanks to improved gold production, lower costs, and record gold prices, which is why this stock is a buy, in our view. Alamos Gold plans to repurchase more shares between December 24, 2025, and December 23, 2026, than in 2025 under its previous share repurchase program: 18.58 million Class A shares versus 1.33 million shares for C$54.4 million at an average price of C$40.97 per share or approximately $29.7542 on December 23, 2025. If the company continues to implement and enhance this share repurchase program despite the expected strong share price appreciation due to the positive correlation with the price of gold, we believe that these market valuations of AGI shares on the NYSE represent a buying opportunity relative to the value the company will return to the market in the near future as its business will generate robust cash flows. Alamos Gold has received approval from the Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX) to repurchase its own shares. The positive effects of this strategy are expected to be felt not only on the TSX but also on the NYSE, as this share buyback program will be possible due to the continued strong cash flow or value.

The Risk

The assets are located in mining-friendly regions and do not pose a material threat to the continuation of Alamos' mining and exploration activities in Canada and Mexico. The only risk is the price of gold: While the strong correlation between AGI's share price and the price of gold could lead to price fluctuations, an abrupt trend reversal in the gold price would have a significant adverse impact on the expected growth of cash flow from the company's operating activities and on its ongoing share buyback program. This risk is very low given the current situation, as macroeconomic problems, exacerbating geopolitical tensions, and the projected interest rate cuts by the US Federal Reserve in 2026 should drive the price of gold higher going forward.

Conclusion

Alamos Gold Inc. is a Canadian gold producer with production and expansion activities in Canada and Mexico—two regions ideally suited for onshore mining and exploration. Alamos's cash flow benefits from record gold prices, rising gold production, and decreasing costs. The shares have significant upside potential, as the market will—as is typical—react positively to Alamos' plans for capital distribution through share buybacks and dividends. This is the strongest evidence of the value the company can offer in the market.

data.title: The Trade Desk: The Death Of Its Growth Is Exaggerated
data.description:
Overview

The Trade Desk (TTD) had a rough year in 2025; the stock ended ~70% lower for the year. The company achieved revenue growth rates in the second and third quarters of 2025 that were materially lower than its five-year average. Wall Street is panicking that growth will be structurally lower from now on, primarily because of an increased threat from Amazon.

Wall Street is concerned because Amazon is now selling advertisements not just on its own properties, like Amazon.com and Amazon Prime, but also on other companies' platforms. This expansion means Amazon is starting to compete more directly with TTD by moving into the same areas of digital advertising.

Although some of these arguments warrant some vigilance, I am bullish on the stock. Amazon is not a full substitute for TTD because TTD has two main advantages: its neutrality and its breadth. Additionally, CTV advertising expenditure, where TTD makes 50% of its money, is nowhere near saturated, and audio is becoming the next big growth driver.

Company Overview

TTD is a platform where advertisers can buy ads. It is the biggest independent ad-buying platform where advertisers buy ads on streaming television, audio, display ads, and in-app advertising. TTD doesn't own any content; it's merely a technology platform, and it earns money by charging a take rate on the advertising spend on its platform.

The two main competitors are Google's DV360 and Amazon's ad buying platform called Amazon DSP (demand-side platform, a platform to buy ads). As I will show in this article, TTD has distinct advantages against these two because of its breadth and its neutrality.

Growth Appears to Be Hit by Macro Headwinds

As you can see from the chart below, revenue growth bottomed out in the third quarter of 2025 at 17.7%. In Q2 it was slightly higher. The median revenue growth rate over the last five years was 25%, so this is the reason Wall Street is nervous.

If we listen carefully to Jeff Green, CEO of TTD, he does mention that advertising spend is influenced by macro volatility. Per Q2 2025 earnings call:

"From a macro standpoint, some of the world's largest brands are absolutely facing pressure and a degree of uncertainty. Some must respond more than others to tariffs. Many are managing inflation worries and the related pricing that comes with that."

In Q3 2025, he mentions that "especially retail and consumer products are still feeling pressure from inflation and tariffs."

TTD is a company that is sensitive to macro shocks because the spending that happens on their platform is discretionary. The nature of CTV advertising on the platform is that brands want to create brand awareness and reach. Like advertisements on analog TV, the return on investment is not instant but shows up over time.

This is different from Google Search ads or Amazon.com ads, where users show clear buying intent. If an advertiser were to stop advertising spending on Google Search or Amazon ads, sales would probably immediately be affected.

But with TTD, that is not immediately the case, so it's easier for CFOs or chief marketing officers to hold off on spending on TTD's platform if macro uncertainties arise.

Furthermore, TTD has shown sensitivity to macro shocks in the past as well. The prime example is in Q2 2020, when revenue growth suddenly contracted by -13%. That was also a situation where TTD reacted more heavily to macro shocks than Google or Meta. Google's revenue fell 1.7% that quarter, while Meta's grew 10.7%.

The last crucial point is that, excluding political advertising spending in Q3 2025, the revenue growth rate would be 22%. Excluding political spending makes sense because revenue was artificially high in Q3 2024 due to the US elections. A 22% growth rate would be perfectly normal for Trade Desk.

For me, the explanation that management has given seems plausible, given the nature of the business and historical sensitivity to macro shocks.

The CTV Engine is Still Running

Let's look at TTD's revenue growth drivers. CTV (connected television or streaming TV) advertising makes up about half of TTD's business, so it's important to track its status. The graph below shows that CTV ads account for 32% of total US TV spending. Compare that to 40 to 60 percent of users already watching CTV rather than analog TV, and the takeaway is that most TV budgets still need to migrate to CTV, which will benefit TTD.

Looking at the chart, an additional $5 billion annual spend will move over from linear TV spending to CTV. That is $5 billion potential to flow through TTD's platform. On a trailing 12-month revenue of $2.8 billion, $5 billion of potential spend can be a significant revenue driver.

Two main reasons that support the increase in CTV advertising spend are:

  • CTV players like Disney and Netflix have successfully introduced ad tiers. That increases (premium) CTV ad availability and may attract more new advertisers.
  • CTV advertising is also becoming much more performance-based, meaning that the return on investment is becoming much clearer versus traditional TV advertising. For example, by using TTD's developed Unified ID technology, advertisers can see if a user who watched a CTV advertisement also bought something at Walmart. That's because Unified ID is a method where email addresses get encrypted and tokenized so that offline purchases can be linked to a CTV ad, usually by linking the logged-in email address of the CTV service with an offline loyalty card.

All taken together, the remaining market opportunity, continued growth in ad-tier models, and better performance measures all support continued growth in CTV advertising.

Joint Business Plans at All-Time Highs

Other evidence of future growth is indicated by CEO Jeff Green's statement that joint business plans (JBP) are at record highs, as mentioned in the Q3 2025 earnings call:

"We are signing more multiyear JBPs, or joint business plans, than ever before with leading agencies and brands. In fact, the number of live JBPs is at an all-time high, and we continue to see spending under JBPs significantly outpaced the rest of our business. What is even more encouraging is the strength of our JBP pipeline, with 100 JBPs in progress, many of them in the late stages of development."

Growth in JBPs is a bullish sign because:

  • It creates predictability on the amount of spending the customer is planning to make.
  • Increases the stickiness of the customer. By signing a JBP, it's less likely that the client is moving away advertising spend to a competitor.
  • It's usually also a sign that a brand is willing to commit its data to the TTD platform. Like the example with Walmart, where its store shopping data is used to link to CTV ads.

Audio Advertising is the Next Growth Driver for TTD

Another major growth avenue in the coming years can be audio-not traditional radio, but digital audio such as Spotify, Pandora, iHeart, and similar platforms. TTD has integrations with all of them. This market is rapidly shifting toward programmatic advertising. As recently as April 2025, Spotify introduced its Spotify Ad Exchange, enabling advertisers to bid programmatically on Spotify's audio inventory.

Specifically, demand for podcast advertising is enormous. TTD estimates that consumers spend an average of three hours a day listening to their favorite music and podcasts. Their forecast is that audio and CTV will increase as a percentage of the revenue mix.

According to Digiday, programmatic buys on Spotify alone grew 58% year over year in Q3 2024. Currently, most podcast ads are pre-recorded and therefore not tailored to the individual listener. Programmatic audio advertising enables ads to be served dynamically to specific listeners, significantly increasing value for advertisers compared to pre-recorded ads.

Considering how much time people spend listening to audio while driving, exercising, or taking a walk, I agree with Green that audio is likely to become the largest component of the revenue mix.

According to the chart below from eMarketer, programmatic audio spend is expected to reach $2.6 billion in 2026, representing a 17% CAGR since 2022.

Audio has a big future, considering the engagement audio has during the day. One research study in Australia, for example, finds that digital audio has a higher engagement than CTV:

While audio currently represents only about 5% of TTD's business, the key takeaway is that audio is likely to become a meaningful growth driver-much like CTV has been in recent years-further cementing TTD's long-term growth runway.

Amazon's Conflict of Interest is Its Weakness

A concern I often see among analysts and fellow writers is the perceived threat from Amazon's own DSP (demand-side platform) and, to a lesser extent, Google's DSP. Amazon operates its own DSP, allowing advertisers to buy ads directly through its platform. The issue is that Amazon and Google face a conflict of interest: they own both the buying platform and the inventory. Amazon simply earns more if they sell you an ad on their own platform (Amazon.com, Fire TV) compared to an external publisher because they don't have to pay an intermediary.

TTD does not have websites or CTV channels where you can buy ads. It simply routes ad spend to the placements that deliver the highest value for the advertiser. This is a key difference between Amazon and TTD and why TTD has a structural advantage.

Here are two examples where TTD's neutrality shows up in the customer experience:

  • Reporting: TTD provides log-level data for ad campaigns, offering detailed insight into when an ad was served, on which website or app, at what price, and to which ID. You can see the cost to the publisher separate from the TTD fee and any other line items. Amazon puts everything in a single figure; you can't know for sure how much of it went to a publisher and what part went to Amazon.
  • Bid Transparency: Another benefit of TTD's neutrality is that it compares your bid to the clearing price-the price actually paid for an advertisement. This lets you see how much you over- or underpaid. This is especially important because the ad tech industry has shifted to first-price auctions in recent years. This means that if bidder A pays $10 and bidder B $8, A pays $10 for the ad. In a second-price auction, A would just pay $8.01.

You can see that Amazon playing referee and player simultaneously is hurting the customers' ability to assess its ROI when buying ads.

Why TTD's Reach Matters

In addition to its neutrality, TTD profits from its fast breadth of connections with publishers worldwide. While Amazon has made deals with Netflix, Roku, and major news sites, Amazon is more selective in its reach. It can reach off-platform inventory, but it tends to focus on a smaller set of premium publishers. TTD also has a far wider reach internationally.

Its greater reach is an advantage for campaigns that need to run across multiple channels. Advertisers have a higher probability of reaching users throughout the day by combining CTV, audio, display, mobile, and other channels. With Amazon, this is harder because its (global) reach is less.

Because of its reach, TTD participates in millions of ad impressions per second, allowing it to identify cheaper ads. TTD has a better view of "ad liquidity" in the system, thereby having a better chance to retrieve the actual price of an ad. Its AI model, Kokai, has more data to work with and is able to offer the customer more efficient pricing than Amazon, which focuses on a more select set of publishers.

In short, TTD benefits from two major structural advantages: its neutrality and its breadth.

Where Amazon Does Compete

Amazon is strong when the shopper journey happens on Amazon. If a user browses on Amazon, searches for a product, or adds something to their cart, that information is valuable to an advertiser. If that same user then watches content on Prime Video and later purchases something on Amazon.com, advertisers can clearly see that the ad worked. That is gold for marketers and is known as closed-loop attribution.

However, when a user does not have a meaningful "Amazon footprint," the value of the Amazon DSP declines.

Since Amazon is still a small part of the global retail landscape, advertisers will therefore continue to use both platforms together: Amazon DSP for high-quality purchase data within the Amazon ecosystem and TTD for reaching users across the open internet.

Risks

The two main risks for the thesis are most probably:

Prolonged macro weakness in advertising spend.

If the recent slowdown in growth is indeed macro-driven rather than structural, a more prolonged period of macro weakness could continue to pressure TTD's growth rates. Historically, TTD's revenues have shown sensitivity to macro shocks.

Walled gardens expanding access to third-party inventory

This would occur if Amazon or Google increasingly offered ad inventory on properties other than their own. In that case, TTD's moat-its reach-would weaken. That scenario is unlikely since it is in the interest of Amazon to sell high-margin ad inventory on their own platforms rather than ad inventory on other platforms.

Conclusion

The prevailing market narrative that TTD's growth rates are structurally being affected by Amazon is flawed. Revenue appears to have been hit by 2025 macro volatility rather than competitive pressure. Amazon is primarily interested in selling ads on their own properties, which is precisely what its DSP is built to do. And Amazon properties are just a slice of the open internet.

What really sets TTD apart from Amazon is its neutrality and breadth. The stock is trading at multi-year lows, and even a move back toward peer-average valuations would imply 45-50% upside. Any reacceleration in growth would be additional upside on top of that. Given the remaining market opportunity in CTV and the advent of audio, this is perfectly achievable. The risk-reward is highly attractive. I rate the stock a strong buy.
